figures.evidence_labeling: Imported structure/surrogate evidence and synthetic computational evidence are labeled by result origin; synthetic inputs are not biological measurements.
figures.mechanical_conclusion: 7T10 supports a contact-localized tensile mechanics interpretation: a small hotspot set concentrates structural anchoring while the force trace passes a linear tensile-response gate with measurable stiffness, work, and peak force.
panels: Contact hotspot concentration with entropy, Gini, and load-anchor index.
Force-extension trace comparing accepted linear tensile model against rejected mean-force descriptor.
AIC gate showing why extension-dependent tensile response is retained.
Mechanics claim, stress-test meaning, and regime-transition residual content.
figures.publication_significance: Figure emphasizes accepted model, rejected alternative, gate or stress test, and mechanics claim.
figures.title: 7T10 contact-localized tensile mechanics

figures.evidence_labeling: Imported structure/surrogate evidence and synthetic computational evidence are labeled by result origin; synthetic inputs are not biological measurements.
figures.mechanical_conclusion: The fiber-network run supports an anisotropic mechanics claim: orientation eigenstructure defines a dominant load-bearing axis while the stress-strain surrogate supplies the tensile stiffness scale.
panels: Stress-strain accepted model compared with isotropic descriptor.
Perturbation stress test and mechanics claim.

figures.mechanical_conclusion: The mechanobiology run supports a graph-mediated load-routing claim: traction is best explained as a force-path property combining adhesion, cytoskeletal coupling, displacement, and path length, not as adhesion alone.
panels: Full force-path model motivation versus adhesion-only ablation.

figures.mechanical_conclusion: The membrane run supports a curvature-energy mechanics claim: the synthetic curvature field has localized bending-energy structure whose total magnitude is controlled by the assigned bending modulus.
